target.title: browser.proxy: allow overriding the browser-control port for split/remote gateways
target.body:
`BrowserProxyCapability` hard-derives the browser-control host as `gatewayPort + 2` on `127.0.0.1`. That is correct for a co-located gateway, but breaks for a **split/remote gateway** — e.g. the gateway running in WSL2 with the Companion on the Windows host, where the browser-control host is reached on a different local port (an SSH-forwarded port). Every `browser.proxy` request then fails as unreachable at the derived port.

This adds one optional override — `BrowserControlPort` — so the Companion can target the actual forwarded browser-control port. **Default is unchanged**: when unset, the port is derived exactly as today (`gatewayPort + 2`).

The browser-control **auth token is unchanged** — it continues to come from the per-gateway shared token (`record.SharedGatewayToken`) via the existing credential path, so this PR adds **no new credential storage** and stays within the `GatewayRegistry` credential model.

## Changes
- `OpenClaw.Shared/SettingsData.cs` — add `BrowserControlPort` (int?). Null (default) = derive as today.
- `OpenClaw.Tray.WinUI/Services/SettingsManager.cs` — forwarder.
- `OpenClaw.Shared/Capabilities/BrowserProxyCapability.cs` — ctor gains `controlPortOverride`; `TryResolveControlEndpoint` honours an explicit port (TCP-range validated) else `gatewayPort + 2`.
- `OpenClaw.Tray.WinUI/Services/NodeService.cs` — pass the setting into the capability ctor.
- `tests/OpenClaw.Shared.Tests/CapabilityTests.cs` — +3 `[Fact]`: port override targets the configured port; out-of-range port errors cleanly; and a real-HTTP round-trip proving the override directs genuine traffic.

## Scope boundary
- Port-only: a port is not a credential, so this stays clear of the `GatewayRegistry` credential boundary.
- Settings-editable; no Settings UI added (advanced/optional override).
- No gateway-side change; Companion-only.

## Tested
- `dotnet test tests/OpenClaw.Shared.Tests` → **2268 passed, 0 failed, 29 skipped**.
- **Real behaviour proof (not just unit logic):** one of the new facts, `BrowserProxy_ControlPortOverride_RealHttpRoundTripHitsOverridePort`, drives the actual capability with a **real `HttpClient`** (no mock handler) against a **real loopback HTTP server**. The gateway URL's port (18790) would derive control port 18792 — the wrong, unreachable port in a port-remapping tunnel — and the test asserts the request actually arrives over a real socket at the override port, carrying the per-gateway shared token, and returns 200. So the override is shown to direct genuine TCP/HTTP traffic, not just build a URL string.
- `dotnet build src/OpenClaw.Tray.WinUI -c Debug -r win-x64` → all assemblies compile.

## Not tested
- No Settings UI, so no UI test.
- Not captured against a production remote gateway in CI — the real-HTTP integration test exercises the actual socket path on loopback; the motivating real-world repro was a WSL2 ↔ Windows packet capture showing the request failing to reach the host at the derived port.

## Risk / mitigation
- *Misconfigured port* → out-of-range is validated and returns a clear error; unset falls back to today's `gatewayPort + 2`.
- *No credential change* → the auth-token path is untouched (per-gateway shared token), so there is no new secret storage or gateway-switch credential concern.

---
*Supersedes the initial revision, which also added a global `BrowserControlToken` setting. Dropped after review: current source already sends the per-gateway shared token (`record.SharedGatewayToken`) to the browser-control host (`NodeService` comment + ctor), so the token override was redundant — the only genuine gap was the hard-coded control port.*
target.author: anagnorisis2peripeteia
